identity.title: Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling

lens.included_activities: Independent providers that repeatedly sell marketing research, audience measurement, survey design, fieldwork, panels, data collection, analysis, or public-opinion research to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate research teams, one-off internal projects, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, media, political, and institutional customers buy recurring tracking, panel, measurement, fieldwork, or repeat research engagements, usually through project, retainer, subscription, or panel-access fees.

a.rationale: S2 identifies data collection, analysis, processing, survey-method design, and report preparation as core work. AI can substitute for portions of coding, synthesis, tabulation, draft reporting, and routine desk research, while research design, sampling validity, interpretation, client context, and accountability constrain the wage-weighted opportunity.
a.change_evidence: Firm-level time sheets by role and workflow, plus a task-level pilot that records baseline hours, quality defects, and post-deployment staffing, would materially refine the estimate.
caveats: O*NET covers a national occupation rather than all occupations within NAICS 541910.
The estimate excludes work that is already automated and does not assume that task exposure equals realized savings.

rho.rationale: The exposed workflows are largely digital and can be redesigned, but S2 also emphasizes exactness, analytical judgment, external communication, and research-method decisions. Client confidentiality, respondent privacy, sampling controls, and reproducibility make implementation materially slower than mere tool availability.
rho.change_evidence: Documented adoption rates and realized-hours data from comparable research agencies, together with client data-processing agreements and quality-assurance logs, would materially move this value.
caveats: This is a five-year operational judgment, not a forecast of vendor capabilities.
Implementation will vary substantially between panel operators, analytics shops, and political pollsters.

e.rationale: S1 defines the industry around gathering, recording, tabulating, and presenting marketing and opinion data, activities commonly sold to external users. The estimate removes establishments whose work is principally episodic, captive, or otherwise fails the recurring-or-repeat outsourced-service lens.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level revenue-recurrence screen using financial statements, CRM exports, and customer-concentration schedules for the LMM population would materially change the estimate.
caveats: NAICS classification does not report customer recurrence or EBITDA.
Political polling and bespoke project work can have materially less repeat revenue than audience measurement or panel businesses.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability is bounded judgment because no retrieved source measures qualifying transactions for the LMM subset of this six-digit industry. S4 establishes that Census publishes employer-owner age characteristics, which is relevant succession evidence but does not itself measure transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal transaction dataset filtered to NAICS 541910 and an LMM revenue or EBITDA band, combined with founder age and succession interviews, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Owner retirement, business closure, and a qualifying control transfer are different events.
The estimate excludes internal reorganizations and does not assume an age distribution from all small businesses applies to this industry.

q.rationale: S5 documents a service mix spanning research, sampling, and data collection. Retained benefit should be higher for differentiated panels, measurement assets, and fixed-fee managed programs, but competitive rebids, hourly or project pricing, and customer expectations of lower research cost should share a meaningful portion of productivity gains.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts showing renewal terms, rate cards, labor-indexation clauses, and win-loss evidence after automation would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The product classification describes services, not contract economics.
The estimate does not assign retention from software savings to demand expansion or volume changes.

d5.bridge: S3 measures national employment for Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists across all employing industries and projects 7 percent growth from 2024 to 2034. It is converted into a five-year directional proxy for constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 541910 service basket. Because the occupation is employed broadly outside this industry and employment can diverge from service quantity as productivity changes, the proxy can overstate or understate the target construct.
d5.rationale: S3 attributes projected occupational growth to increased use of data and market research. That supports a modest central demand-growth assumption, while the lower bound recognizes self-service analytics, platform data, and customer budget pressure that can displace outsourced current-basket work.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census services receipts by detailed product, customer research-budget surveys, and renewal volumes from LMM providers would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This is not an industry revenue forecast.
The occupation projection extends beyond the five-year horizon and includes in-house roles, consulting, and other employers.

o.rationale: S1's industry definition still requires systematic collection and presentation of data, and S2 shows tasks involving research-method design, interpretation, communication, and judgment. Software can self-serve routine dashboarding and simple surveys, but accountable providers should remain necessary for defensible sampling, difficult fieldwork, panel operations, data governance, and consequential interpretation.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews that distinguish self-serve analytics from externally accountable research purchases, plus vendor retention and replacement data, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate concerns the current service basket rather than all future information products.
The need for an operator is likely lower for commoditized survey tools and higher for regulated, high-stakes, or proprietary-panel work.

narrative.executive_view: Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling comprises systematic gathering, recording, tabulating, and presentation of marketing and opinion data [S1]. The lens centers on external providers with recurring or repeat customer work; the key tension is substantial workflow digitization alongside rapid commoditization of routine research outputs.
narrative.ai_impact: Core analyst work includes collecting and analyzing customer and market data, designing collection methods, preparing reports, and communicating results [S2]. AI is most relevant to data preparation, coding, synthesis, draft reporting, and routine research, while sample validity, interpretation, client context, and quality control remain operator responsibilities.
narrative.value_capture: The product taxonomy includes research, sampling and survey-design, and data-collection services [S5]. Managed panels and differentiated measurement can retain productivity gains, whereas project bids, hourly work, and increasingly capable self-service tools make repricing and customer sharing likely.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry definition covers externally supplied research services [S1], but the screen excludes captive and purely one-off work. Transfer evidence is not available at the requested industry and LMM scope; Census owner-age tables are a useful diligence input but do not identify qualifying sales [S4].
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects 7 percent growth in market-research-analyst employment from 2024 to 2034 and cites increased use of data and market research as the driver [S3]. That is supportive but imperfect evidence because the occupation is spread across the economy and does not directly measure outsourced service quantity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Self-service survey platforms, generative analysis, and clients' own data teams can reduce purchases of the current service basket. Privacy, respondent-data governance, sampling bias, research reproducibility, client confidentiality, and the episodic nature of some polling work can also limit implementation and transferability.
narrative.principal_driver: Digital research workflows create a meaningful opportunity to reduce repetitive data and reporting work while preserving accountable research delivery.
narrative.principal_weakness: Direct evidence on LMM recurrence, qualifying transfers, contract pass-through, and industry-specific constant-quality demand is sparse.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 541910 Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling
sources.supports: Official industry definition and included activities.

sources.title: Census Annual Business Survey: 2022 Characteristics of Business Owners
sources.supports: Availability and reference-year scope of employer-business owner-age data for succession diligence.

sources.title: Census NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54191
sources.supports: Detailed product scope for marketing research, sampling, survey design, and data-collection services.
